text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 19 November 2016 

Dr Priya Menon
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Menon
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Ward, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K742193 NHS No. 9075143321  
12 Brookfield Close, London, SE15 3AB

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in clinic. He attended with his partner and brought notes from a local peer support group, who have been observing his recent events alongside the family. These collateral observations have been particularly helpful in corroborating the temporal pattern and recovery features reported at home.

He describes brief episodes with a sudden halt in conversation or activity, a fixed stare, and purposeless fidgeting with both hands. These episodes may be accompanied by a short vocalisation at onset. According to the patient, his partner, and peers from the support group, the events tend to cluster rather than occur singly. Specifically, they report: Weekly clusters, usually 6 or more events within ~2 h. Between clusters he may have single odd spells of unease and a metallic taste without clear behavioural change. Post-event, he is tired, mildly confused, and complains of a dull frontal headache for up to 30–45 minutes, after which he returns to baseline. There is no consistent nocturnal pattern observed by the household, and no clear diurnal trigger has been identified. He has not sustained tongue biting or injuries. He does not recall the episodes, though he sometimes notices a brief rising epigastric sensation shortly beforehand.

Medication history includes prior trials of levetiracetam (stopped for mood irritability) and lamotrigine (ineffective at standard dosing per patient report). He currently takes brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily and sertraline 50 mg once daily prescribed by his GP for low mood. He denies hazardous alcohol use or recreational drugs. Sleep can be irregular due to shift work. The peer group have encouraged a trigger diary; his entries so far are incomplete but suggest that missed meals and sleep deprivation may precede the weekly clustering. He has not yet used a wearable seizure-alert device but is open to it.

Examination today was unremarkable with normal speech, cranial nerves, limb power, coordination, and gait. No focal neurological deficits were identified. Routine bloods from last month (FBC, U&E, LFTs, glucose) were reportedly within reference ranges as per the GP summary. He has not yet had prolonged ambulatory EEG or inpatient monitoring.

At this stage I am still consolidating the semiology and event pattern and will need further objective data. I have discussed obtaining prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture typical clusters and to clarify the nature of these events. He understands the rationale and is agreeable. I have also suggested continuing the trigger diary, with input from the peer support group to document timing, duration, automatisms, awareness, and recovery for each event in a cluster, along with any potential precipitants (sleep loss, missed meals, stress). We will also request prior imaging from the regional hospital; if unavailable or outdated, a 3T MRI epilepsy protocol would be appropriate. No medication changes were made today.

I will update you once monitoring is arranged and we have captured representative events. In the meantime, he and his partner have an agreed safety plan for bathing, cooking, and commuting, and the peer group will continue to provide collateral observations which are proving valuable.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Weekly clusters, usually 6 or more events within ~2 h.
Weekly clusters, usually 6 or more events within ~2 h

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes clusters of events: "Weekly clusters, usually 6 or more events within ~2 h." This provides both the cluster frequency (weekly) and the number of events per cluster (usually 6 or more). No other higher-frequency pattern is specified. Therefore, the normalized label should reflect 1 cluster per week with 6 or more per cluster. Using allowed format with a range/threshold, we represent '6 or more' as '6 to multiple' per cluster.
seizure_frequency_number: Weekly clusters, usually 6 or more events within ~2 h.
reference: Weekly clusters, usually 6 or more events within ~2 h
